title: Digestive biscuits
subtitle: Wholemeal oat biscuit with a light sweetness and sandy crumb
excerpt: Homemade digestive biscuits use wholemeal flour, porridge oats, and a small amount of plain flour with butter, sugar, and milk to make a dough that rolls and cuts cleanly. The baked biscuits are harder and more textured than the bought version and taste significantly more wheaty. Good with cheese as well as the usual tea pairing.
sourceNotes: The digestive biscuit was developed in the 1830s in Britain, originally marketed on the claim that bicarbonate of soda aided digestion (a claim long abandoned). Alexander Grant, a doctor working for McVitie's, is credited with developing the recipe that became the McVitie's Digestive in 1892, which remains the British standard. The home-made version uses wholemeal flour and rolled oats in proportions close to the original commercial formula. The bicarbonate of soda provides a very slight lift and a mild tang.

content.text: than the bought biscuit. The dough is quite dry and stiff when first mixed; it will come together as it rests. Do not add more milk to soften it; the biscuits will spread and lose their shape. Roll the dough thin (3 to 4 mm) for a crispier result; thicker biscuits take longer to bake through.

Blitz the porridge oats in a food processor until roughly broken down to a coarser flour (some texture should remain). Tip into a large bowl with the wholemeal flour, plain flour, bicarbonate, salt, and sugar. Rub in the cold butter until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Add the milk and bring together into a firm dough. If the dough crumbles, add the milk a teaspoon at a time. Wrap in clingfilm and rest for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Roll the dough out to 3 to 4 mm on a lightly floured surface. Cut into 7 cm rounds using a cutter or glass. Prick each biscuit all over with a fork. Arrange on lined baking trays and bake for 12 to 15 minutes until a deep golden brown. They will firm as they cool. Transfer to a rack after 5 minutes.
